event: Portrait of Saint John the Baptist with the Lamb
symbols: lamb at his feet — the Agnus Dei, the Lamb of God that John proclaimed Jesus to be
cross-topped staff with scroll reading ECCE AGNUS DEI — Behold the Lamb of God, John's defining proclamation
camel-hair garment — John's ascetic wilderness life, fulfilling the prophetic tradition
finger pointing upward — directing attention away from himself toward heaven and Christ
baptism scene in background — John's ministry of baptizing in the Jordan, preparing the way for the Messiah
description: A full-length portrait of John the Baptist standing barefoot in a rocky landscape. He wears a rough camel-hair garment belted at the waist, with a radiant golden halo around his head. His right hand points upward while his left holds a tall cross-topped staff from which a scroll or banner unfurls with the inscription 'ECCE AGNUS DEI' (Behold the Lamb of God). A small white lamb stands at his feet on the left. In the background to the right, a luminous scene shows the baptism of Christ in the Jordan with a radiant dove descending from heaven.
theological_description: This portrait of John the Baptist presents the forerunner of Christ with all his traditional attributes, each rooted in Scripture. The camel-hair garment fulfills Mark 1:6 and Matthew 3:4, connecting John to the prophetic tradition of Elijah (2 Kings 1:8). His declaration 'Ecce Agnus Dei' on the scroll banner comes from John 1:29: 'Behold the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world' — the single most important Christological identification in the Gospels, linking Jesus to the Passover lamb of Exodus 12 and the suffering servant of Isaiah 53:7 who was led like a lamb to the slaughter. The finger pointing upward embodies John's self-effacing theology expressed in John 3:30: 'He must increase, but I must decrease.' The baptism scene in the background depicts the moment in Matthew 3:16-17 when the heavens opened and the Spirit descended like a dove. John stands as the hinge between the Old and New Testaments — the last and greatest of the prophets (Matthew 11:11) whose entire purpose was to prepare the way of the Lord (Isaiah 40:3).

scripture_refs.reason: Behold the Lamb of God — the inscription on the scroll and John's defining proclamation
John's camel-hair clothing and leather belt — the garment depicted in the painting
He must increase, I must decrease — the upward-pointing finger directing attention to Christ
The voice crying in the wilderness, prepare the way of the Lord — John's prophetic identity
The baptism of Christ with the descending dove — the background scene
